sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on Custard Apples Australia, Queensland and Western Australian government horticulture, the Australian custard apple industry, and the Rare Fruit Council of Australia archives.

core.intro: Whatever the label on the pot, the fruit sold as custard apple in Australian nurseries is almost always the atemoya, a hybrid of the sugar apple (Annona squamosa) and the cherimoya (Annona cherimola). The cherimoya is a separate, cooler-climate fruit and the sugar apple its small tropical cousin, but all three are Annona species with the same creamy, sweet flesh, and the atemoya is the one that crops well in Australia's warm coastal districts. Wherever it grows it wants the same things: a warm, frost-free spot, deep free-draining soil, shelter from wind, and steady moisture while the fruit fills. The state sections cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes below apply anywhere custard apple will grow.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: African Pride is the workhorse: a smaller, early variety (about 500 to 800 grams) that bears within two or three years, crops heavily and reliably and sets fruit on its own, though it carries more seed. Pinks Mammoth, and its smoother-skinned strain Hillary White, is the premium type, with very large (to around 3 kg), almost seedless fruit, but it flowers shy, comes into bearing later, and needs hand pollination to crop well. KJ Pink, a newer selection from the Pinks Mammoth line, gives that same large premium fruit but sets heavy crops without hand pollination, which is why it is now the most planted variety. Geffner, an Israeli atemoya, is the easy backyard pick: self-pollinating, low-seed and reliable year to year. Decide whether you want effortless fruit or premium fruit, then match the variety to the in-stock table above.

sections.heading: Pollination and hand pollination
sections.body: Custard apple flowers are an awkward shape and open female first, then male, so a flower rarely pollinates itself and natural set leans on small nitidulid beetles rather than honeybees. A single tree will still set some fruit, so you do not need two trees, but yield and fruit shape lift a lot with help. Commercial growers hand pollinate by collecting pollen from flowers picked the afternoon before, then dusting it onto the female-stage flowers with a small brush in the early morning; warm, humid, still mornings (above about 70 percent humidity) give the best set. Hand pollination is essential for a good crop of Pinks Mammoth and Hillary White, optional on the self-fertile African Pride, which is also the preferred source of pollen, and largely unnecessary on the naturally fruitful KJ Pink and Geffner.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Custard apple needs full sun, deep free-draining soil and shelter, because it has brittle wood and will not tolerate waterlogging, which quickly rots the roots. Avoid low, wet spots, plant on a mound if your soil is heavy, and give the tree a windbreak: wind snaps limbs, strips flowers and lowers the humidity the tree wants at flowering. Young trees are frost-tender and need a warm, protected position. Container-grown trees go in best through the warmer months once the soil has warmed (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Water matters most from flowering right through to harvest: the tree needs steady, even moisture while the fruit is filling, both to size the fruit and to stop it splitting, and it wants the equivalent of at least 700 mm of rain a year, so irrigate through any dry spell. Ease right off in winter, when the tree is semi-deciduous and resting. Feed regularly with a balanced fertiliser, building up the dose as the tree grows and putting most of it on after harvest and through the spring flush; a foliar dose of boron at flowering is a grower's trick that improves fruit set and shape. Keep a thick mulch over the shallow roots and off the trunk.

sections.heading: Harvest and ripening
sections.body: Atemoyas take roughly 20 to 25 weeks from pollination to maturity and the main crop ripens through autumn and winter. Pick the fruit mature but still firm, when the skin lightens from deep green to a paler, creamy green and the segments flatten and fill out; it then softens to ripe on the bench over a few days, giving slightly to gentle pressure. The fruit bruises easily and does not store long, so handle it gently and keep ripe fruit only a few days, and not too cold, since temperatures below about 8 degrees cause chilling damage. A grafted tree usually starts bearing in about two to three years.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named, grafted tree. Atemoyas are grafted onto seedling atemoya or cherimoya rootstock, and a grafted tree fruits to type in a couple of years, where a seedling is a gamble that can take four to six years and may never fruit well. Match the variety to how much work you want: Geffner, KJ Pink or Golden Emperor for low-fuss, self-setting fruit, or Pinks Mammoth and Hillary White if you want the biggest, best fruit and are happy to hand pollinate. If you like the family, the related Brazilian custard apple (rollinia) is worth a look too. The in-stock table above shows the named varieties available now and what they cost.

faqs.q: Is a custard apple the same as a cherimoya or a sugar apple?
faqs.a: They are close cousins, not the same. In Australia, custard apple almost always means the atemoya, while the cherimoya is a distinct fruit that likes cooler, higher country, and the sugar apple (or sweetsop) is the small, knobbly tropical one. They look and taste similar, but they are sold as different trees, so check the label for the name you actually want.

faqs.q: How big does a custard apple tree get, and can I keep it small?
faqs.a: Left alone an atemoya makes a spreading tree around 5 to 8 metres tall with long, low branches, but it is easily held to a backyard-friendly 3 to 4 metres. Prune it while it is bare in late winter to shape an open framework, then tip-prune through the season, which also makes the fruit far easier to reach, bag and pick.

faqs.q: Can I grow a custard apple in a pot?
faqs.a: Yes, atemoyas take well to a large container, which suits a courtyard or a tree you can move under cover in a cold or wet snap. Use a deep, free-draining mix, water through summer because pots dry out fast, feed regularly as nutrients wash through, and choose a naturally compact, self-fruitful variety such as Geffner or Golden Emperor so you are not hand pollinating a potted tree.

faqs.q: Why has my custard apple fruit turned black?
faqs.a: Blackened skin or hard, mummified fruit is usually anthracnose, a fungal disease that thrives in warm, wet, humid weather and is the main reason backyard fruit fails on the coast. Keep the canopy open and airy, clear away fallen and mummified fruit so the fungus cannot carry over, and in a wet season a registered fungicide from flowering through fruit set helps. Good airflow and tidy hygiene do most of the work.

WA.intro: Growing custard apple in Western Australia. WA is a minor custard apple area, a few percent of the national crop, and the limit is not heat but water and frost: the warm north is very dry, and the cooler south-west can frost young trees.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Custard apple needs warmth, freedom from frost and plenty of moisture. The Gascoyne and the Kimberley have the warmth and are frost-free, but they are dry, so a tree there lives entirely on irrigation. The Mediterranean south-west, including Perth, has the rainfall but cool, wet winters and the risk of frost on young trees, so it grows custard apple only in warm, sheltered, frost-free pockets.

sections.heading: Where custard apple grows in WA
sections.body: What little WA grows is in the warm, frost-free north: the Carnarvon district on the Gascoyne coast and the irrigation country around Kununurra in the Kimberley, both wholly reliant on irrigation. Further south, around Gingin and in sheltered Perth gardens, it is a backyard curiosity rather than a crop.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: There is no set WA season the way there is in the east. In the frost-free north the warmth can bring fruit on earlier and over a longer, less defined spread, while a sheltered Perth tree ripens its small crop through the warmer months.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: WA's dry air spares custard apple much of the anthracnose that plagues the humid east. The main pest is Mediterranean fruit fly, which is established across the south-west and stings ripening fruit, while mealybug can also appear. Trees in the hot northern districts must be watched for sunburn on exposed fruit and kept well watered.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA's strict quarantine means live plants from other states must meet the import rules, and many eastern nurseries will not post custard apple trees here. The in-stock table above is filtered to the sellers that can ship to WA, which in practice is mostly WA nurseries plus the few interstate sellers that comply.

faqs.q: Can you grow custard apples in Perth?
faqs.a: Yes, in the right spot. Perth's cool, wet winters and the chance of frost make it marginal, but a warm, sheltered, frost-free corner, a sunny north-facing wall or a paved courtyard, will grow a backyard tree, and a self-fruitful variety like Geffner keeps it simple. Keep the water up over the long dry summer.

faqs.q: Which custard apple varieties suit Western Australia?
faqs.a: In the frost-free north around Carnarvon and Kununurra, the reliable commercial types like African Pride and KJ Pink do best under steady irrigation. For a backyard tree in the south-west, a compact, self-pollinating variety such as Geffner or Golden Emperor is the safe pick, since you will not want to hand pollinate a lone tree.

QLD.intro: Growing custard apple in Queensland. Queensland is the heart of the Australian industry: the fruit is grown right along the warm coast, from the Sunshine Coast hinterland in the south-east, the largest production area in the country, north to the Atherton Tablelands, with pockets through the Wide Bay and Central Queensland.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Custard apple loves Queensland's warm, frost-free, humid coast. The trees flower and set fruit best in the warm, moist conditions of a coastal summer, which is why the subtropical south-east and the elevated tropical Tablelands outperform both the cool inland and the hot, wet far-north lowlands. Frost is the limit: it cuts back young trees, so even in Queensland the coldest inland valleys are marginal.

sections.heading: Where custard apple grows in QLD
sections.body: The Sunshine Coast hinterland behind Nambour and Maleny is the biggest growing district in the country. North of there the Atherton Tablelands grow it in the cooler highlands above the tropical coast, the Wide Bay around Bundaberg and Childers adds to the crop, and the warm Yeppoon district in Central Queensland can claim the first fruit of the season.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: The main Queensland crop runs from about February through winter to September, with the warm Central Queensland district picking earliest and the south-east carrying the bulk of the autumn and winter fruit. Newer varieties such as PinksBlush stretch the supply on into spring.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Queensland's warmth and humidity bring the main troubles. Queensland fruit fly stings ripening fruit and is worst through late summer. Fruit spotting bug pierces young fruit, causing lumps, dimples and fruit drop, and is one of the worst custard apple pests. Mealybugs shelter between the segments and are spread by ants, and anthracnose, a fungal disease, blackens fruit in wet weather. Keep the canopy open, manage ants and stay on top of fruit fly.

faqs.q: Which custard apple varieties suit southeast Queensland?
faqs.a: For the subtropical south-east, the easy choices are African Pride and the newer KJ Pink, which crop reliably without much fuss, while Geffner makes a good low-maintenance backyard tree. Keen growers add Pinks Mammoth or Hillary White for premium fruit and hand pollinate them. All do well given sun, shelter and sharp drainage.

faqs.q: Can you grow custard apples in tropical north Queensland?
faqs.a: Yes, but choose your spot. In the humid far-north lowlands the trees grow well but disease pressure is high, so the cooler Tablelands and the drier inland give better fruit, and the small, knobbly sugar apple is the Annona that really suits the hot tropics. On the wet coast, keep the canopy open and airy and expect to manage anthracnose.

NSW.intro: Growing custard apple in New South Wales. The warm, subtropical far-north coast of NSW is the single biggest custard apple region in the country, centred on the Northern Rivers, and the crop thins out as you move south down the coast.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: The far-north coast of NSW has the warm, frost-free, humid summers custard apple wants, which is why so much of the national crop grows there. The trade-off is disease: the same coastal humidity that helps the fruit set also feeds anthracnose, and inland or further south the winters turn too cool and frosty for reliable cropping.

sections.heading: Where custard apple grows in NSW
sections.body: The Northern Rivers around Lismore is the core district, and the state's tropical fruit research centre at Alstonville sits in the middle of it. Plantings run north toward the Tweed and south down the Coffs Harbour coast to about Stuarts Point, which marks the southern edge of the commercial range.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: The NSW crop comes in mainly through autumn and winter, roughly February to September, with the warm coastal districts holding fruit well into the cooler months. The far-north coast, being a touch warmer than inland, tends to lead the local picking.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Anthracnose is the big one on the humid NSW coast: the Alstonville and Tuckombil district is a known hot spot for it, with the disease easing as you move to drier inland air. Queensland fruit fly and fruit spotting bug both attack the fruit, and mealybugs ride in on ants, so an open canopy, clean-up of fallen fruit and ant control all earn their keep.

faqs.q: Which custard apple varieties grow in the Northern Rivers?
faqs.a: The Northern Rivers grows the full commercial range. African Pride and KJ Pink are the mainstays for reliable, low-input crops, Pinks Mammoth and Hillary White are grown for premium fruit with hand pollination, and Geffner suits a low-fuss home garden. Choose an open, airy canopy and tidy orchard hygiene to cope with the coastal humidity.

faqs.q: How far south can you grow custard apple along the coast?
faqs.a: Reliable cropping runs out south of the Coffs Harbour coast and the Stuarts Point area; beyond there the winters get too cool and frost-prone. In a warm, sheltered, frost-free pocket on the mid-north or central coast you can still grow a backyard tree, but treat any fruit as a bonus and keep it clear of cold, wet hollows.

VIC.intro: Growing custard apple in Victoria. Custard apple is not a Victorian crop. The state is too cool and too frost-prone for the tree to flower and ripen a reliable crop outdoors, so here it is a pot or greenhouse project rather than a backyard fruit tree.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Custard apple is subtropical and frost-tender, and it needs a long, warm, frost-free season to carry fruit through the 20-odd weeks from flowering to harvest. Victoria's cool summers and cold, frosty winters cut that short: young trees are damaged by frost, and the long ripening would run into the cold, so Australia's custard apple growing stops well to the north, in New South Wales.

sections.heading: If you still want to try
sections.body: Grow a dwarf, self-fruitful variety such as Golden Emperor in a large pot, keep it in the warmest, most sheltered corner of a Melbourne garden or in a greenhouse, and move it under cover for winter. Expect leafy growth far more often than fruit. For a dependable home crop a hardier subtropical like feijoa is a surer bet, and custard apple is best bought as fruit in season.

faqs.q: Will a custard apple fruit in Melbourne?
faqs.a: Almost certainly not outdoors. Melbourne is too cool and too frosty: the tree may survive in a warm courtyard or greenhouse, but it will rarely flower and ripen fruit, because the long warm season custard apple needs simply is not there. Grow it in a pot for the foliage and the novelty, and buy the fruit from the northern crop.

faqs.q: Is it worth planting a custard apple outside in Victoria?
faqs.a: Not as a fruit tree. The mix of frost and a short, cool season means an outdoor tree will struggle to crop, even on the warm coast or in Gippsland. A dwarf in a large pot that you can shift under glass or against a hot north wall for winter is the only sensible way to grow one this far south, and even then fruit is a long shot.
